s: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid — the pyramid was built around 2560 BCE, while Cleopatra died in 30 BCE, just 1,999 years before Apollo 11.
q: Cleopatra lived closer in time to which event?
d: The invention of the printing press
e: The Great Pyramid was already over 2,500 years old when Cleopatra was born. We tend to compress ancient history, imagining pyramids and pharaohs as one era, but more time separates the pyramids from Cleopatra than separates Cleopatra from us. The T-Rex was closer in time to seeing a Stegosaurus than to us too.

s: Coca-Cola did not invent the modern image of Santa Claus — red-suited Santas appeared in illustrations decades before Coca-Cola's 1931 ad campaign, including in Harper's Weekly and on Coca-Cola's own earlier ads.
q: Did Coca-Cola invent the image of Santa Claus in a red suit?
a: No — red-suited Santas appeared decades earlier in other publications
d: Partially — Coca-Cola modified an earlier design
The image existed but was refined by Coca-Cola
Coca-Cola invented the costume, not the concept
No — Santa was always depicted in white
e: The myth that Coca-Cola created Santa's red suit is one of the most persistent in pop culture. Santa had been depicted in red since at least the 1870s by Thomas Nast. What Coca-Cola's artist Haddon Sundblom did was standardize a warm, grandfatherly version that became culturally dominant, but the red suit predates them.

s: Coco Chanel popularized the 'little black dress' in 1926, at a time when black clothing was primarily associated with mourning.
q: Who popularized the 'little black dress' in 1926?
e: Vogue published Chanel's simple black dress design and predicted it would become 'a sort of uniform for all women of taste.' The LBD transformed black from a color of grief into one of elegance and versatility.

s: Being cold doesn't cause the common cold — colds are caused by viruses, primarily rhinoviruses. The winter association exists because people crowd indoors, making virus transmission easier.
q: What actually causes the common cold?
a: Viruses, primarily rhinoviruses — not cold temperatures
e: Studies where volunteers were chilled or exposed to cold weather showed no increase in cold infection rates. However, a 2023 study found that cold air does reduce the nasal immune response slightly, which could make you marginally more susceptible if already exposed to a virus. The main driver of winter colds is indoor crowding and dry air.

s: Confirmation bias is the tendency to search for, interpret, and remember information that confirms one's preexisting beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence.
q: What cognitive bias describes the tendency to favor information that confirms existing beliefs?
e: First described by psychologist Peter Wason in the 1960s, confirmation bias affects decision-making in everything from politics to medical diagnoses. People unconsciously give more weight to information that supports what they already believe.

s: Convento de Las Mínimas de Jerez de la Frontera is a Catholic convent in Jerez.
q: What is Convento de Las Mínimas de Jerez de la Frontera?
e: Convento de Las Mínimas de Jerez de la Frontera represents an important cultural and architectural landmark. Sites like this one connect us to the beliefs, traditions, and artistic achievements of earlier civilizations.

s: The Guinness World Record for the largest collection of traffic cones belongs to David Morgan of the UK, who amassed over 500 unique cones.
q: What is David Morgan's Guinness World Record for the largest collection of?
e: Morgan, a traffic management professional, spent decades collecting traffic cones of different designs, colors, and eras from around the world. His collection includes rare vintage cones from the 1950s.

s: In poker, the 'dead man's hand' — two pair of aces and eights — is named after Wild Bill Hickok, who was allegedly holding these cards when he was shot dead in 1876.
q: The 'dead man's hand' in poker is named after which historical figure?
e: James Butler 'Wild Bill' Hickok was playing poker in a Deadwood, South Dakota saloon when Jack McCall shot him from behind. The cards he was holding became legendary, though the exact fifth card is debated.

s: The Dead Sea is so salty (about 34% salinity) that you can effortlessly float on its surface while reading a newspaper — it's nearly 10 times saltier than the ocean.
q: Approximately how much saltier is the Dead Sea compared to typical ocean water?
e: The extreme salinity is caused by water flowing in but having no outlet except evaporation, concentrating minerals over thousands of years. The Dead Sea is also the lowest point on Earth's land surface, at about 430 meters below sea level. It's shrinking rapidly — its surface has dropped about 35 meters since 1960 due to water diversion.

s: Dendera Temple complex is described as ancient Egyptian temple complex.
q: What type of ancient Egyptian structure stands at Dendera?
e: A breathtaking temple preserving irreplaceable cultural heritage

s: Diamonds are not actually rare — they're one of the most common gemstones. Their perceived scarcity was artificially created by De Beers, which controlled the supply and invented the 'a diamond is forever' marketing campaign in 1947.
q: Why are diamonds perceived as rare despite being relatively common?
a: De Beers artificially restricted supply and created marketing campaigns
d: They're actually mined in very small quantities globally
Mining technology can't extract them easily
They only form under extremely rare conditions
e: De Beers stockpiled diamonds and restricted supply to keep prices high. The idea that diamonds are essential for engagement rings was also a marketing invention — before De Beers' campaign, engagement rings featured various gemstones. Russia and Botswana have enormous diamond deposits. Lab-grown diamonds are chemically identical to natural ones and cost 60-80% less.

s: Digos Conventual Priory is a Roman Catholic monastery in Davao del Sur, Philippines.
q: What is Digos Conventual Priory?
a: Roman Catholic monastery in Davao del Sur, Philippines
e: Digos Conventual Priory represents monastic life and spiritual dedication. Monasteries like this one serve as centers of learning, contemplation, and preservation of religious traditions across generations.

s: Dunbar's number suggests humans can maintain stable social relationships with approximately 150 people, based on the size of our neocortex.
q: Dunbar's number proposes that humans can maintain stable relationships with approximately how many people?
e: British anthropologist Robin Dunbar proposed in the 1990s that primate brain size correlates with social group size. For humans, the cognitive limit for meaningful relationships is roughly 150.

s: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with limited knowledge in a domain tend to overestimate their own competence in that domain.
q: What cognitive bias causes people with low ability to overestimate their competence?
e: Psychologists David Dunning and Justin Kruger published this finding in 1999. They showed that unskilled individuals not only make poor decisions but also lack the metacognitive ability to recognize their errors.

s: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability at a task overestimate their competence, while experts tend to underestimate theirs.
q: What does the Dunning-Kruger effect describe?
a: Low-skill people overestimate their ability while experts underestimate theirs
d: High-skill people are always confident while novices doubt themselves
All people overestimate their ability equally
Experts tend to overestimate while novices underestimate
Low-skill people always know they're bad at tasks
e: Psychologists David Dunning and Justin Kruger demonstrated in 1999 that incompetent people lack the metacognitive skill to recognize their own incompetence. Conversely, skilled people assume tasks easy for them are easy for everyone.

s: The Dvorak keyboard layout, patented in 1936, places the most common English letters on the home row, but never displaced the QWERTY layout.
q: Who patented the Dvorak keyboard layout in 1936?
e: August Dvorak designed his layout to reduce finger travel and increase typing speed. Despite studies showing potential efficiency gains, QWERTY's entrenchment and switching costs kept Dvorak a niche alternative.

s: E-ninnu is a temple located in Girsu.
q: Where is E-ninnu located?
e: E-ninnu stands as a timeless testament to ancient architectural mastery

s: Eanna is a temple located in Uruk.
q: Where is Eanna located?
e: This remarkable temple has witnessed centuries of human history unfold

s: Days were only about 23 hours long when dinosaurs roamed the Earth — the Moon's tidal forces have been gradually slowing Earth's rotation, making our days longer by about 2.3 milliseconds per century.
q: Approximately how long was a day when dinosaurs lived, about 70 million years ago?
e: Fossil coral growth rings confirm shorter days in the past. In the Cretaceous period (about 70 million years ago), a year had roughly 372 days instead of 365, each about 23.5 hours long. Eventually, in billions of years, Earth's rotation will slow enough that one day will equal one month.

s: Earth's rotation is slowing by about 2.3 milliseconds per century, meaning dinosaurs experienced roughly 23-hour days 200 million years ago.
q: Approximately how much is Earth's day lengthening per century?
e: Tidal interactions between Earth and the Moon create friction that gradually slows Earth's spin. Fossil evidence from coral growth rings and tidal records confirms that days were shorter in the geological past.

s: You're currently hurtling through space at about 1,670 km/h due to Earth's rotation (at the equator) and at 107,000 km/h around the Sun — but you feel nothing because everything around you moves at the same speed.
q: Why don't you feel the Earth rotating at 1,670 km/h?
a: Because everything around you moves at the same constant speed — you only feel acceleration, not velocity
d: Because you're moving too fast to feel anything
Because Earth's gravity masks the sensation
Because your body has adapted to the speed
Because the atmosphere cushions the motion
Because you're in a vacuum-like environment
Because the core protects you from rotation
e: On top of orbital motion, the entire solar system moves at about 828,000 km/h around the Milky Way, and the galaxy itself moves at about 2.1 million km/h relative to the cosmic microwave background. You don't feel any of this because there's no acceleration — it's constant velocity. You only feel changes in speed, not speed itself.

s: The Eiffel Tower grows by up to 15 centimeters in summer due to thermal expansion — the iron heats up, causing the metal to expand, making the tower measurably taller on hot days.
q: By how much can the Eiffel Tower grow in hot summer weather?
e: Iron expands when heated, and the Eiffel Tower contains 7,300 tonnes of it. On very hot days, the temperature difference between the sun-facing and shaded sides can also cause the top to lean away from the sun by up to 18 centimeters. The tower was originally intended to be demolished after 20 years but was saved because it was useful as a radio antenna.

s: Esperanto, the world's most widely spoken constructed language, was created by L. L. Zamenhof in 1887 with the goal of fostering international peace.
q: Who created Esperanto in 1887?
e: Zamenhof, a Polish-Jewish ophthalmologist, grew up in a multilingual city with ethnic tensions. He designed Esperanto to be easy to learn, with regular grammar and no irregular verbs. Today, an estimated 100,000–2,000,000 people speak it.

s: Ethiopia uses a calendar that is roughly 7-8 years behind the Gregorian calendar, with 13 months — twelve of 30 days and one of 5 or 6 days.
q: How many months does the Ethiopian calendar have?
e: The Ethiopian calendar is based on the older Alexandrian calendar. The gap exists because Ethiopia did not adopt the Dionysian calculation for the birth year of Jesus that most of the world follows.

s: The word 'salary' comes from the Latin 'salarium,' which referred to the allowance given to Roman soldiers to buy salt.
q: What commodity is the English word 'salary' etymologically linked to?
e: Salt was extremely valuable in ancient Rome for preserving food. Roman soldiers received a 'salarium' — a salt allowance — which eventually became synonymous with regular payment for work.

s: Daniel Fahrenheit originally set 96 degrees as human body temperature on his scale, using his wife's temperature — the modern 98.6°F is a later adjustment.
q: What did Daniel Fahrenheit originally set as body temperature on his scale?
e: Fahrenheit calibrated his 1724 scale with three reference points: freezing brine (0°F), freezing water (32°F), and body temperature (96°F). The 98.6°F value came from a later conversion of 37°C and has itself been questioned by modern studies.

s: The fax machine was invented in 1843 by Alexander Bain — 33 years before Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone in 1876.
q: The fax machine was invented how many years before the telephone?
e: Bain's 'Electric Printing Telegraph' could transmit images over telegraph lines using a scanning mechanism. While primitive compared to modern fax machines, the underlying concept predated the telephone by over three decades.

s: Modern Olympic fencing swords are wired with electronic sensors, and the scoring system uses what is essentially a very early form of networking — electrical signals travel through the blade, through the fencer's body wire, to a central scoring box.
q: How does electronic scoring work in Olympic fencing?
a: Sensors in the blade complete an electrical circuit when the target is hit with sufficient force
d: A pressure pad under the mask detects hits
A laser beam interrupts when touched
Weight sensors in the footwork area
e: Electronic scoring was introduced in 1933 for epee and later for foil and sabre. The point of the weapon is spring-loaded; when it's depressed with enough force (750 grams for foil, 750g for epee), it completes a circuit. In foil and sabre, the valid target area is defined by a metallic vest (lame) worn over the uniform that completes the circuit only when hit in the right zone.

s: The Fibonacci sequence was introduced to Western mathematics through a problem about rabbit breeding in Fibonacci's 1202 book Liber Abaci.
q: The Fibonacci sequence was introduced through a puzzle about what in Liber Abaci (1202)?
e: Leonardo of Pisa (Fibonacci) posed a puzzle about how many pairs of rabbits would exist after a year if each pair produces a new pair monthly. The resulting sequence (1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13...) appears throughout nature.

s: Koala fingerprints are so similar to human fingerprints that they have occasionally confused forensic investigators at crime scenes — even under a microscope, they're nearly indistinguishable.
q: Which animal has fingerprints so similar to humans that they can confuse forensic investigators?
e: Koalas are not closely related to primates at all — they're marsupials. Their fingerprints evolved independently through convergent evolution, likely because both humans and koalas needed enhanced grip. This is remarkable because no other non-primate mammal has such human-like fingerprints.

s: The term 'computer bug' was popularized in 1947 when engineers found an actual moth trapped in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer.
q: What insect was famously found inside the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947?
e: Grace Hopper's team taped the moth into their logbook with the note 'First actual case of bug being found.' While the term 'bug' for technical glitches predates this event, it cemented the word in computing culture.

s: The first email was sent by Ray Tomlinson in 1971, and he chose the @ symbol to separate the user name from the computer name.
q: Who sent the first email in 1971 and introduced the @ symbol for email addresses?
e: Tomlinson, an ARPANET engineer, sent the first network email between two computers sitting side by side. He selected @ because it was rarely used and clearly meant 'at' a particular machine.

s: The world's first narrative feature film is generally considered to be 'The Story of the Kelly Gang' (1906), produced in Australia.
q: Which country produced what is generally considered the world's first narrative feature film in 1906?
e: Running over 60 minutes, this Australian film about the outlaw Ned Kelly predates Hollywood's first features by several years. It was added to UNESCO's Memory of the World Register in 2007.

s: The oldest surviving photograph, 'View from the Window at Le Gras,' was taken by Nicéphore Niépce around 1826–1827 and required an 8-hour exposure.
q: Who took the oldest surviving photograph, and roughly how long was the exposure?
d: William Henry Fox Talbot, about 20 minutes
e: Niépce used a pewter plate coated with bitumen of Judea to capture the view from his estate. The extremely long exposure time meant the sunlight appears on both sides of the buildings as the sun moved across the sky.

s: Fortune cookies were invented in California by Japanese Americans, not in China — they are virtually unknown in China today.
q: Where were fortune cookies actually invented?
e: The most supported theory attributes fortune cookies to Makoto Hagiwara of San Francisco's Japanese Tea Garden around 1914, or Seiichi Kito in Los Angeles. Chinese restaurants adopted them in the 1940s when Japanese Americans were forced into internment camps during WWII.

s: Forum Temple of Emporiae is a temple.
q: What type of Roman religious structure stood in the forum of Emporiae?
e: Forum Temple of Emporiae represents an important cultural and architectural landmark. Sites like this one connect us to the beliefs, traditions, and artistic achievements of earlier civilizations.

s: The 2015 French-language Scrabble world champion, Nigel Richards from New Zealand, doesn't speak French — he memorized the entire French Scrabble dictionary without understanding any of the words.
q: What remarkable feat did Nigel Richards accomplish at the 2015 French Scrabble championship?
a: Won the title without speaking French, having memorized the dictionary
d: Broke the world speed record for language memorization
Won without studying the rules at all
Defeated the defending champion in French
Set a record for highest score ever achieved
e: Richards, widely considered the greatest competitive Scrabble player ever, memorized approximately 386,000 French words in just nine weeks to compete in the Francophone championship. He had already won the English-language World Scrabble Championship multiple times. He reportedly approached it as a purely mathematical puzzle.

s: The 'boiling frog' metaphor is scientifically false — a frog placed in gradually heated water WILL try to escape once it gets uncomfortable. The myth has been debunked by biologists multiple times.
q: What actually happens when a frog is placed in slowly heated water?
a: It tries to escape once the water gets uncomfortably warm
d: It stays in the water and boils to death
It gradually stops moving until it's immobilized
It loses consciousness and cannot react
Its reflexes become too slow to escape
e: The metaphor claims frogs won't notice gradually increasing water temperature and will boil to death. In reality, frogs have thermoreceptors and will jump out when the water gets too hot. The story was popularized in the 19th century from a poorly conducted experiment. Modern biologists, including Douglas Melton of Harvard, have confirmed frogs will escape.

s: Garni Temple is described as sacred architecture.
q: What type of structure is Garni Temple?
e: Garni Temple offers a window into a fascinating lost civilization

s: The concept of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) was developed by economist Simon Kuznets in 1934 for a US Congress report.
q: Who developed the concept of GDP in 1934?
e: Kuznets created national income accounting to help measure the impact of the Great Depression. He later warned that GDP should not be used as a measure of welfare, a caution largely ignored by policymakers.

s: Goldfish actually have memories lasting months, not seconds — scientists have trained them to push levers, navigate mazes, and recognize their owners, debunking the '3-second memory' myth.
q: How long can goldfish actually remember things, according to scientific studies?
e: Researchers at the University of Plymouth trained goldfish to press a lever for food and found they remembered how months later. Israeli scientists trained goldfish to respond to sounds and the memory lasted at least five months. The '3-second memory' myth has no scientific basis and likely arose from a misunderstanding or joke that got repeated as fact.

s: GPS satellites must account for Einstein's theory of relativity to stay accurate — without relativistic corrections, GPS positions would drift by about 10 kilometers per day.
q: By approximately how much would GPS positions drift per day without relativistic corrections?
e: Two effects are at play: special relativity makes the satellites' clocks tick slower (by 7 microseconds/day) because they're moving fast, while general relativity makes them tick faster (by 45 microseconds/day) because they experience weaker gravity. The net effect is clocks running 38 microseconds fast per day, which must be corrected or positions would be wildly wrong.

s: The Great Wall of China is NOT visible from space with the naked eye — this is one of the most widespread myths in the world. Astronauts confirm it's too narrow (about 5 meters wide) to see from orbit.
q: Can the Great Wall of China be seen from space with the naked eye?
a: No — it's far too narrow to distinguish from orbit
d: Visible if you know where to look
e: The myth predates spaceflight, appearing in print as early as 1754. Chinese astronaut Yang Liwei confirmed he couldn't see it from space in 2003. At its widest, the wall is about 5 meters — from orbit, that's like trying to see a single hair from 3 kilometers away. Highways and airports are much more visible.

s: If Greenland's ice sheet melted completely, it would raise global sea levels by about 7.4 meters (24 feet) — enough to submerge most coastal cities on Earth.
q: By how much would global sea levels rise if Greenland's entire ice sheet melted?
e: Greenland's ice sheet contains roughly 2.85 million cubic kilometers of ice and is up to 3 kilometers thick. It's the second-largest ice body on Earth after Antarctica. The ice sheet has been losing mass at accelerating rates since the 1990s, losing about 270 billion tonnes per year.

s: When the Gregorian calendar was adopted in 1582, ten days were skipped — October 4 was followed directly by October 15 to correct accumulated drift.
q: How many days were skipped when the Gregorian calendar was first adopted in 1582?
e: The Julian calendar had overestimated the year by 11 minutes, causing it to drift 10 days out of sync with the solar year by the 16th century. Pope Gregory XIII ordered the skip, and different countries adopted the change over the next 340 years.

s: The # symbol is officially called an octothorpe, a name coined by Bell Labs engineers in the 1960s when adding it to telephone keypads.
q: What is the official name of the # symbol, coined by Bell Labs?
e: While the exact origin of 'octothorpe' is debated (possibly honoring Olympic athlete Jim Thorpe, or simply meaning 'eight points'), Bell Labs engineers needed a unique name for the symbol when they added it to touch-tone phones.

s: The Hawthorne effect describes how people modify their behavior when they know they are being observed, regardless of any actual changes to their environment.
q: What is the Hawthorne effect?
a: People change behavior when they know they're being observed
d: The tendency to overestimate small probabilities
Anxiety caused by new work environments
Increased productivity from better lighting alone
Loss of motivation after repeated study
Adaptation to sensory stimuli over time
e: Named after experiments at the Hawthorne Works factory in the 1920s–30s, workers improved productivity when lighting was increased AND when it was decreased — simply because they knew they were being studied.

s: Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible — honey is one of the only foods that never spoils if stored properly.
q: What property of honey allows 3,000-year-old samples found in Egyptian tombs to remain edible?
a: Its low moisture, high sugar, and natural antibacterial properties prevent spoilage
d: Its amber color reflects harmful radiation
e: Honey's eternal shelf life comes from a perfect storm of chemistry: its low moisture content (around 17%) starves bacteria, its high sugar concentration creates osmotic pressure that kills microbes, and bees add an enzyme (glucose oxidase) that produces small amounts of hydrogen peroxide. Its acidity (pH 3-4) is also inhospitable to most organisms.

s: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with bananas — not because we're closely related, but because many fundamental cellular processes (like cell division and energy production) use the same ancient genetic code.
q: Approximately what percentage of DNA do humans share with bananas?
e: DNA similarities reflect shared ancestry billions of years ago when all life descended from common single-celled organisms. We share about 96% with chimpanzees, 90% with cats, 85% with mice, 60% with chickens, 60% with bananas, and about 44% with fruit flies. The shared genes handle basic cellular housekeeping that evolution had no reason to change.

s: IKEA is an acronym formed from the founder's initials (Ingvar Kamprad), the farm where he grew up (Elmtaryd), and the nearby village (Agunnaryd).
q: What does the acronym IKEA stand for?
e: Ingvar Kamprad founded IKEA in 1943 at age 17. The name combines I(ngvar) K(amprad) E(lmtaryd) A(gunnaryd), making it a deeply personal acronym rather than a Swedish word.
